entry.title: Inter-Level Cooperation in Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning
entry.content:
这篇文章的思想是将multi-agent和HRL相结合来解决high-level的non-stationary的问题，旨在通过high-level和low-level之间的交流，使high-level在学习策略的时候将low-level的表现考虑进去。

而这篇论文的实现手法也很简单粗暴，将low-level k steps的cumulative reward加入high-level的objective中：

实验：

能看出来比HIRO的效果要好，但是没有和HAC相比较，所以也不知道这种解决non-stationary的方式和hindsight这种relabel的方式相比，哪种效果更好。

entry.title: non stationary in RL
entry.content:
最近看了一些关于RL中所面临的不稳定问题（non-stationary）的文章，所谓的non-stationary就是MDP中的transition和reward都会随着时间或缓慢或突然发生变化，这样agent学习的策略将不再适用于新的环境模型，对于一些off policy或者offline的方法，增加了更多的困难。

发现大多数的方法采用的都是：

  1. 检测环境变化点，即由一个环境模型变化为另一个环境模型的转换点，检测方式往往采用的是利用数据分布的比较。
  2. 采用forget或者restart的方式，重新学习策略，以应对新的环境模型。这点虽然看起来十分低效，但我觉得为了防止过往数据经验对新策略学习的影响，restart够简单粗暴，而且有效。就像我们训练一个网络，我们肯定不希望数据中的random label的fake data对模型的训练带来不好的影响，所以对这些数据的剔除，不失为一个好办法。

也有人结合最近的lifelong 和 meta-learning来学习策略，这样的模型利用过往的经验加速了对新环境的适应过程，但同时也增加了模型的计算和存储要求。

在众多方法中，我觉得窗口滑动和RUQ方法结合或许可以有不错的效果（虽然两个方法都很早了。。）：

窗口滑动类似于forget，这使agent摆脱了过往错误数据对其策略学习的影响，但是本身重新开始的学习又显得不是很efficiency。RUQ的思想很简单，就是对于概率很低的动作，更新多次。有些状态动作因为其概率较低，所以我们很可能无法reach，所以就很难更新其value。RUQ就是当reach到这个状态动作时，一次性进行多次的更新，来防止一种explore和exploit之间的不平衡。

entry.title: 分层强化学习
entry.content:
分层强化学习

本质上，为了选择适当的层次结构框架，需要知道域知识的可用程度（三者的组合也是可以想象的）：

  • 如果行为是完全指定的：选项框架
  • 如果行为被部分指定：HAM 框架
  • 如果较少的域知识可用：MAXQ、学习选项框架

在下面的表中，列方向分别表示：

  • 时间抽象：允许表示在不同的时间尺度上发生的动作方案的知识。我们讨论的是时间延长的动作。
  • 状态抽象：当子任务忽略环境状态的某些方面时，它要求以终止语句的形式指定子任务，而不是使用选项或部分策略方法。
  • “子任务：程序员提供的固定策略”意味着给定一组选项，系统通过这些选项学习策略。
  • “子任务：非确定性有限状态控制器”意味着给定部分策略的层次结构，系统学习整个问题的策略。
  • “子任务：终止语句和局部奖励函数”意味着给定一组子任务，系统学习整个问题的策略。
  • 分层最优策略是一种策略，它在给定层次结构的所有可表达的策略中是最优的。
  • 递归最优策略是指每个 SMDP（半马尔科夫决策过程）的策略最优，对应于分解中的每个子任务。

从根本上讲，HRL 的优势在于：通过解决扩展问题而更快的学习、通过减少维数灾难解决大的状态 - 动作空间问题的强大能力、在不同的抽象状态任务上使用子目标和抽象的动作，使用多层次的时间抽象，更真实和更好的泛化能力，从之前的任务中迁移知识，这些似乎已经触手可及，但还没有完全实现。

有很多很棒的想法和算法，但到目前为止还没有产生很大的影响，或广泛采用。坦率地说，RL 社区仍然存在合理的怀疑主义。一方面，例如通过更新层次代理的级别所产生的非平稳性等挑战，需要在实现上做出更多的努力，还需要引入额外的超参数。另一方面，我们还远没有达到合理的样本效率，正如 Fruit 等人在论文《Regret Minimization in MDPs with Options without Prior Knowledge》中所指出的：在选项框架中，选项是原子相互独立的宏动作。因此，我们失去了考虑选项之间的相互作用的潜在好处，而它可以显著提高样本效率。此外，如 OpenAI 博客文章中所述：“RL 研究者（包括我们自己）普遍认为，**长期的发展还需要本质上的进展，如分层强化学习。**我们的结果表明，我们没有给予如今的算法合适的评价——至少在他们有足够的规模和合理的探索方式的情况下。

除非 HRL 框架具有更强的用户友好性和更好的技术规范，HRL 将不会成为 RL 问题的标准。

也就是说，这些缺陷是与 HRL 的主要研究方向一致的已知问题，包括但不限于：

  • 高阶状态迁移函数非平稳性的更好管理
  • 自动学习层次结构
  • 更有效的探索
  • 丰富稀疏奖赏环境下有效分解的信号
  • 提高稳定性

我们已经发现深度网络中出现的分层结构可应用于感知任务，如计算机视觉中的卷积神经网络，但这些是感知层面上的分层。如果我们可以以同样的方式在 RL 中构建层次化的时间和行为系统，这将像卷积神经网络一样产生重大影响。目前仍有许多工作要做，并且还需要一个坚实的基础。

Background

Reinforcement learning

We assume the interaction between agent and environment can be modeled as a {Markov decision process} (MDP). An environment is described by a set of states SSS, a set of actions AAA, a distribution of initial states p(s0)p(s_0)p(s0​), a reward function r:S×A→Rr : S\times A \to \mathbb{R}r:S×A→R, transition probabilities p(st+1∣st,at)p(s_{t+1}\mid s_t,a_t)p(st+1​∣st​,at​), and a discount factor γ∈[0,1]\gamma \in [0,1]γ∈[0,1], so we refer to this MDP as M≐(S,A,r,p,p0,γ)M \doteq (S, A, r,p,p_0,\gamma)M≐(S,A,r,p,p0​,γ).

A policy π(at∣st)\pi(a_t \mid s_t)π(at​∣st​) that maps from current state sts_tst​ to a probability distribution over actions π:S→A\pi:S \to Aπ:S→A . Each episode starts with sampling an initial state s0s_0s0​. According to the policy π\piπ, the agent takes an action at=π(st)a_t=\pi(s_t)at​=π(st​), then enters into a new state st+1s_{t+1}st+1​ sampled from the transition distribution p(st+1∣st,at)p(s_{t+1}\mid s_t,a_t)p(st+1​∣st​,at​), and receives a reward rt=r(st,at)r_t=r(s_t,a_t)rt​=r(st​,at​). A discounted sum of future rewards is called a {return}: Rt=∑i=t∞γi−triR_t = \sum^{\infty}_{i=t}\gamma^{i-t}r_iRt​=∑i=t∞​γi−tri​, the objective of the agent is to maximize its expected return Es0[R0∣s0]\mathbb{E}_{s_0}[R_0\mid s_0]Es0​​[R0​∣s0​]. The Q-function is defined as Qπ(st,at)=E[Rt∣st,at]Q^{\pi}(s_t,a_t) = \mathbb{E}[R_t\mid s_t,a_t]Qπ(st​,at​)=E[Rt​∣st​,at​].

When the state space is large, or not all states are observable, we can use a nonlinear function approximator, such as neural networks, or a linear approximation to calculate Q(s,a;ω)Q(s,a;\omega)Q(s,a;ω), and estimate the Q-function parameterized by ω\omegaω. Let π∗\pi^*π∗ denotes an optimal policy i.e. Qπ∗(s,a)≥Qπ(s,a)Q^{\pi^*}(s,a)\ge Q^{\pi}(s,a)Qπ∗(s,a)≥Qπ(s,a) for every s∈S,a∈As\in S, a\in As∈S,a∈A and any policy π\piπ. It is easy to show that it satisfies the following equation called the {Bellman equation}:

Q^*(s,a) = E_{s'\thicksim p(\cdot\mid s,a)}[r(s,a)+\gamma \max_{a'\in A}Q^*(s',a')].\label{eq1}

We can find the optimal value function by minimizing the loss function L(ω)L(\omega)L(ω), which is defined as the expectation of squared TD error over a recent experience replay DDD :

L(\omega)\triangleq \mathbb{E}_{e\thicksim D}[(r + \gamma \max_{a'}Q(s',a';\omega)-Q(s,a;\omega))^2].\label{eq2}

Semi-Markov decision process

In the MDP, after selecting an action, the agent will immediately jump to the next state according to the state transition equation ppp. In the semi-Markov decision process (SMDP), the number of steps from the current state to the next state is a random variable τ\tauτ, that is, after selecting an action aaa in a certain state sss, it will transfer to the next state s′s′s′ with a probability after τ\tauτ steps. The state transition probability at this time is the joint probability p(s′,τ∣s,a)p(s′,\tau\mid s, a)p(s′,τ∣s,a) of sss and τ\tauτ. When the value of τ\tauτ is a real value, SMDP constructs a continuous-time-discrete-event system; when the value of τ\tauτ is a positive integer, it is a discrete-time SMDP. For the sake of simplicity, most HRL problems are discussed on discrete-time SMDP.

Goal-conditioned RL

We consider a finite-horizon, goal-conditioned MDP defined as a tuple (S,G,A,P,R,γ)(\mathcal{S,G,A,P,R},\gamma)(S,G,A,P,R,γ), where S\mathcal{S}S is a state set, G\mathcal{G}G is a goal set, A\mathcal{A}A is an action set, P:S×A×S→R\mathcal{P:S\times A \times S}\to \mathbb{R}P:S×A×S→R is a state transition, R:S×A→R\mathcal{R:S\times A}\to \mathbb{R}R:S×A→R is a reward function, and γ\gammaγ is a discount factor.

Universal Value Function Approximators (UVFA)

Universal Value Function Approximators

(UVFA) is an extension of Deep Q Learning (DQN)\cite{mnih2013playing} to the setup where there is more than one goal we may try to achieve. Let G\mathcal{G}G be the space of possible goals. Each goal g∈Gg\in \mathcal{G}g∈G corresponds to some reward function rg:S×A→Rr_g:\mathcal{S\times A}\to \mathbb{R}rg​:S×A→R. Each episode starts with sampling a state-goal pair from some distribution p(s0,g)p(s_0,g)p(s0​,g). The goal stays fixed for the whole episode. At every timestep the agent gets not only the current state but also the current goal π:S×G→A\pi:\mathcal{S\times G \to A}π:S×G→A as input and gets the reward rt=rg(st,at)r_t=r_g(s_t,a_t)rt​=rg​(st​,at​). The Q-function now depends not only on a state-action pair but also on a goal Qπ(st,at,g)=E[Rt∣st,at,g]Q^{\pi}(s_t,a_t,g)=\mathbb{E}[R_t\mid s_t,a_t,g]Qπ(st​,at​,g)=E[Rt​∣st​,at​,g].

Hindsight Experience Replay

Because the reward is sparse, it will inevitably lead to many complete transitions that cannot reach the preset goal, obviously, these trajectories cannot be used for the training of the preset goal. For example, if there is a transition whose last step does not reach the preset goal, then this step will not get a positive reward, but if the goal is changed to the state that has been reached by the last step, then this step will get a positive reward. Beyond this step, the goal of the entire transition should be modified to this state, which is the meaning of hindsight. The reason for this is that no matter what the goal setting is, it will not affect the system dynamics. Taking the same action in the same state will reach the same next state, so we can change the generated trajectory with the goal reset to any value. In this way, the number of successful samples in the experience memory will be expanded.

Temporal abstraction based RL

Option RL

Options provide a framework to model temporally extended actions. Formally, options can depend on the entire history between the time ttt when they were initiated and the current time step t+k,ht:t+k≡statst+1…at+k−1st+kt+k,h_{t:t+k}\equiv s_t a_t s_{t+1}\dots a_{t+k-1}s_{t+k}t+k,ht:t+k​≡st​at​st+1​…at+k−1​st+k​. Let H\mathcal{H}H be the space of all possible histories; an option is defined using the 3-state tuple: o≡⟨Io,βo,πo⟩o \equiv \langle \mathcal{I}_o, \beta_o,\pi_o \rangleo≡⟨Io​,βo​,πo​⟩ where Io⊂S\mathcal{I}_o \subset SIo​⊂S is the set of initial states, βo:H→[0,1]\beta_o:\mathcal{H}\to[0,1]βo​:H→[0,1] is the termination probabilities for each state and πo:H→A\pi_o:\mathcal{H\to A}πo​:H→A is the policy over histories. Assuming that the agent is currently in a certain state, an option is selected. Through the policy of this option, the agent selects an action or another option. If an action is selected, the execution will be transferred directly to the next state; if another option is selected, the new option will be used to continue the selection until an action is finally obtained.

Skill learning

SSS and AAA are random variables for states and actions, respectively; Z∼p(z)Z\thicksim p(z)Z∼p(z) is a latent variable, on which we condition our policy; we refer to a policy conditioned on a fixed ZZZ as a “skill”; I(⋅;⋅)I(\cdot;\cdot)I(⋅;⋅) and H[⋅]H[\cdot]H[⋅] refer to mutual information and Shannon entropy, both computed with base eee. Information-theoretic skill\cite{achiam2018variational} discovery methods seek to find a policy that maximizes the mutual information between SSS and ZZZ. Due to symmetry, this measure can be expressed in the following two forms:

\begin{align} I(S;Z)&=H(Z)-H(Z\mid S) \qquad //reverse \\ &=H(S)-H(S\mid Z) \qquad //forward \label{eq4} \end{align}

Hierarchical Deep Reinforcement Learning

Goal-based DRL

Goal-based Framework


At present, the mainstream goal-based HRL is designed based on the h-DQN and FuN framework, that is, it is divided into two different level controllers. The high-level controller outputs the goal, and the low-level controller uses the state and the goal given by the high-level controller as input to learning a goal-based policy.

High-level controller with policy πθhh(g∣s)\pi^h_{\theta_h}(g\mid s)πθh​h​(g∣s) aims to maximize the external reward and generates a high-level action, i.e. a subgoal gt∼πθhh(g∣st)∈Gg_t \thicksim \pi^h_{\theta_h}(g\mid s_t)\in \mathcal{G}gt​∼πθh​h​(g∣st​)∈G every ccc time steps when t≡0(modc)t \equiv 0(mod\quad c)t≡0(modc), where c>1c>1c>1 is a pre-determined hyper-parameter.

It modulates the behavior of the low-level policy by intrinsic rewards. The low-level controller aims to maximize the intrinsic reward provided by the high-level controller, and performs a primary action at∼πθll(a∣st,gt)∈Aa_t\thicksim \pi^l_{\theta_l}(a\mid s_t, g_t)\in \mathcal{A}at​∼πθl​l​(a∣st​,gt​)∈A at every time step.

Follow current mainstream method HIRO, they consider the high-level policy produces a high-level action(or goal) gt∈Rdsg_t\in \mathbb{R}^{d_s}gt​∈Rds​ by either sampling from its policy or otherwise using a fixed goal transition function gt=h(st,gt,st+1)g_t = h(s_{t},g_{t},s_{t+1})gt​=h(st​,gt​,st+1​), where h(st,gt,st+1)=gt+st−st+1h(s_{t},g_{t},s_{t+1})=g_{t}+s_{t}-s_{t+1}h(st​,gt​,st+1​)=gt​+st​−st+1​. The reward function of the high-level policy is defined as:

\begin{equation} ​ r^h_{ct}=\sum^{ct+c-1}_{i=ct}R(s_i,a_i), \qquad t=0,1,2,\dots, \label{eq5} \end{equation}

which is the accumulation of the external reward in the time interval [ct,ct+c−1][ct, ct+c-1][ct,ct+c−1].

And the intrinsic reward that describes subgoal-reaching performance based on the distance between the current observation and the goal, defined as:

\begin{equation} ​ r^l_t(s_t,g_t,a_t,s_{t+1})=-||s_t+g_t-s_{t+1}||_2. \label{eq6} \end{equation}

Off-policy correction

It is straightforward to see that in HRL, the training of high-level policy depends on the low-level policy, that is, the reward and arrival state in the high-level transition tuple depend on the policy adopted by the low-level controller, which results in the previous transition tuple cannot be used for subsequent training. This problem may cause the high/low level non-stationary under off-policy. To solve this problem, HIRO proposes an off-policy correction method. The high-level action gtg_tgt​ which in the past induced a low-level behavior at:t+c−1∼μlo(st:t+c−1,gt:t+c−1)a_t:t+c−1\thicksim \mu^{lo}(s_{t:t+c−1},g_{t:t+c−1})at​:t+c−1∼μlo(st:t+c−1​,gt:t+c−1​) may be re-labeled to a goal g~t\widetilde{g}_tg​t​ which is likely to induce the same low-level behavior with the current lower-level policy. Thus, they propose to remedy the off-policy issue by re-labeling the high-level transition (st,gt,∑Rt:t+c−1,st+c)(s_t,g_t,\sum R_{t:t+c−1},s_{t+c})(st​,gt​,∑Rt:t+c−1​,st+c​) with a different high-level action g~t\widetilde{g}_tg​t​ chosen to maximize the probability μlo(at:t+c−1∣st:t+c−1,g~t:t+c−1)\mu^{lo}(a_{t:t+c−1}\mid s_{t:t+c−1}, \widetilde{g}_{t:t+c−1})μlo(at:t+c−1​∣st:t+c−1​,g​t:t+c−1​), and the log probability can be computed as:

\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} log\mu^{lo}&(a_{t:t+c−1}\mid s_{t:t+c−1}, \widetilde{g}_{t:t+c−1})\\ &\propto -\frac{1}{2}\sum^{t+c-1}_{i=t}||a_i-\mu^{lo}(s_i,\tilde{g}_i||^2_2+const \label{eq7} \end{aligned} \end{equation}

Even this approximate formula is difficult to solve, so by sampling 10 alternative goals and selecting one of them to maximize the above formula. Two of these ten goals are the original goal gtg_tgt​ and st+c−sts_{t+c}-s_tst+c​−st​, and the other 8 goals are sampled by Gaussian distribution centered on st+c−sts_{t+c}-s_tst+c​−st​.

HAC proposes another method to solve the non-stationary brought by different levels of training, that is, using the idea of hindsight to assume that the low-level policy has reached the optimal. HAC proposes three transitions: (1) {hindsight action transition}. For the non-lowest level, the action space is the state space. If the goal set by the upper level is not reached, the reached state will be regarded as the goal, which is equivalent to the higher-level action of hindsight is done so that the higher-level is trained based on the "optimal" lower-level policy, to temporarily ignore the change of the lower-level policy. (2){hindsight goal transition}. This is what the original HER did. Adjust the goal to solve the problem of sparse reward; (3){subgoal testing transition}. Under the above two adjustments, the transition used by the agent to update is some reachable goals. It may not be achievable for some distant goals. Therefore, a certain probability is used to test whether the lower-level policy can achieve the current goal. When the goal cannot be completed by the lower-level, a penalty reward will be given.

From the Figure above we can see that HAC has a faster convergence rate than HIRO. This situation may caused by that HIRO values sub-goal actions with respect to a transition function that essentially uses the current lower level policy hierarchy, not the optimal lower level policy hierarchy as in HAC approach. Consequently, HIRO may need to wait until the lower level policy converges before the higher level can learn a meaningful policy.

Goal representation

FuN uses as an intrinsic reward the direction taken in a feature space. The features used are those which are useful to the task, they are therefore inherently dependent of the extrinsic reward.

Then, RIG proposes to build the feature space independently from the task with variationally auto-encoder (VAE); but this approach can be very sensitive to distractors (i.e. useless features for the task or goal, inside states) and does not allow to correctly weight features.

Skew-fit recently improves over RIG by weighting rare states, leading to more diverse policies. Both RIG and Skew-fit are efficient in reaching a given state.

The current state-of-art method, Sub-optimal representation learning, bounds sub-optimality of the goal representation with respect to the task, giving theoretical guarantees; the agent can solve the task with a hierarchical policy as precisely as with a simple low-level policy.

Goal generation with adversarial training

In the paradigm of adversarial training, two modules face each other: the first one, the generator, tries to fool the second, the discriminator, which must avoid to be mistaken.

GoalGAN learns to generate increasingly complex continuous goals with a Generative Adversarial Networks (GAN) in order to make the policy progressively learn to go everywhere.

The generator of the GAN learns to produce goals of intermediate difficulty and the discriminator learns to distinguish these goals from others.

Intermediate difficulty is characterized as a goal that an agent achieves from time to time.

In this article, the intrinsic reward relies on an hand-engineered indicator function which attributes a binary reward if the agent is close to a goal.

Similar issue can be found in Self-play HRL.In this article, adversarial method is applied to learn a goal space which is a compressed state space.

During a pre-training step, an agent (generator) tries to go into the state that an other agent (discriminator) went into, whereas the discriminator learns to go into areas the generator cannot reach.

In other words, the generator tries to produce trajectories that the discriminator can not differentiate from its own.

Temporal abstraction based HRL

Option framework}

The {call-and-return} option execution model, in which an agent picks option ω\omegaω according to its policy over options πΩ\pi_{\Omega}πΩ​, then follows the intra-option policy πω\pi_{\omega}πω​ until termination (as dictated by βω\beta_{\omega}βω​, at which point this procedure is repeated. Let πω,θ\pi_{\omega,\theta}πω,θ​ denote the intra-option policy of option ω\omegaω parametrized by θ\thetaθ and βω,ϑ\beta_{\omega,\vartheta}βω,ϑ​, the termination function of ω\omegaω parameterized by ϑ\varthetaϑ. The definition of the option-value\cite{bacon2017option} function can be written as:

\begin{equation} ​ Q_{\Omega}(s,\omega)=\sum_a \pi_{\omega,\theta}(a\mid s)Q_U(s,\omega,a),\label{eq8} \end{equation}

where QU:S×Ω×A→RQ_U:\mathcal{S\times \Omega\times A}\to \mathbb{R}QU​:S×Ω×A→R is the value of executing an action in the context of a state-option pair:

\begin{equation} ​ Q_U(s,\omega,a)=r(s,a)+\gamma \sum_{s'} P(s'\mid s,a)U(\omega,s').\label{eq9} \end{equation}

The function U:Ω×S→RU:\Omega\times \mathcal{S}\to \mathbb{R}U:Ω×S→R is called the option-value function \textsl{upon arrival} (Sutton,1999). The value of executing ω\omegaω upon entering a state s′s's′ is given by:

\begin{equation} ​ U(\omega,s')=(1-\beta_{\omega,\vartheta}(s'))Q_{\Omega}(s',\omega)+\beta_{\omega,\vartheta}(s')V_{\Omega}(s').\label{eq10} \end{equation}

Each time an option ω\omegaω is selected for execution according to the selection policy πΩ\pi_{\Omega}πΩ​, and then the intra-option policy πω\pi_{\omega}πω​ corresponding to ω\omegaω is executed to perform specific actions until termination (controlled by the termination function β\betaβ). Next, using the same idea as actor-critic, the parameters of the termination function and intra-option policy will be optimized to maximize the expected reward function and achieve end-to-end training.

Skill learning

Reverse form of the mutual information

The objective can be derived by expanding the definition of the mutual information in Equation, and then leveraging the non-negativity property of the KL divergence to compute a variationally lower bound:

\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} I(S;Z)&=\mathbb{E}_{s,z\sim p(s,z)}[\log p(z\mid s)]-\mathbb{E}_{z\sim p(z)}[\log p(z)] \\ &\geq \mathbb{E}_{s,z\sim p(s,z)}[\log q_{\phi}(z\mid s)]-\mathbb{E}_{z\sim p(z)}[\log p(z)]\label{eq12} \end{aligned} \end{equation}

where log⁡qϕ(z∣s)\log q_{\phi}(z\mid s)logqϕ​(z∣s) is fitted by maximum likelihood on (s,z)(s,z)(s,z) tuple collected by deploying the policy in the environment.

In DIAYN, they maximize the mutual information between skills and states, I(A;Z)I(A;Z)I(A;Z), to encode the idea that the skill should control which states the agent visits. Conveniently, this mutual information dictates that we can infer the skill from the states visited. To ensure that states, not actions, are used to distinguish skills, we minimize the mutual information between skills and actions given the state, I(A;Z∣S)I(A;Z\mid S)I(A;Z∣S). Viewing all skills together with p(z)p(z)p(z) as a mixture of policies, we maximize the entropy H[A∣S]H[A\mid S]H[A∣S] of this mixture policy. In summary, DIAYN maximize:

\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} \mathcal{F}(\theta)&\triangleq I(S;Z)+\mathcal{H}[A\mid S]-I(A;Z\mid S) \\ &= \mathcal{H}[Z]-\mathcal{H}[Z\mid S]+\mathcal{H}[A\mid S,Z] \label{eq14} \end{aligned} \end{equation}

The first term in Equation encourages prior distribution over p(z)p(z)p(z) to have high entropy, DIAYN fixes p(z)p(z)p(z) to be uniform, guaranteeing that is has maximum entropy. The second term suggests that it should be easy to infer the skill zzz from the current state. The third term suggests that each skill should act as randomly as possible which has the same meaning as maximizing the entropy of the action during the SAC policy training. DIAYN approximates the posterior p(z∣s)p(z\mid s)p(z∣s) with a learned discriminator qϕ(z∣s)q_{\phi}(z\mid s)qϕ​(z∣s), which gives a lower bound on F(θ)\mathcal{F}(\theta)F(θ):

\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} &\mathcal{F}(\theta)=\mathcal{H}[A\mid S,Z]-\mathcal{H}[Z\mid S]+\mathcal{H}[Z] \\ &\geq \mathcal{H}[A\mid S,Z]+\mathbb{E}_{z\sim p(z),s\sim \pi(z)}[\log q_{\phi}(z\mid s)-\log p(z)]\\ &\triangleq \mathcal{G}(\theta,\phi) \end{aligned} \end{equation}

The variational lower bound G(θ,ϕ)\mathcal{G}(\theta,\phi)G(θ,ϕ) is optimized by training the policy π(a∣s,z)\pi(a\mid s,z)π(a∣s,z) using the reward function:

\begin{equation} ​ r_z(s,a)\triangleq \log q_{\phi}(z\mid s)-\log p(z) \end{equation}

The algorithm framework is shown in the Figure. Because policy and discriminator’s adversarial unsupervised RL training form a cooperative game, if the discriminator can better infer skills from the state, the rewards obtained by skills will be more stable, thus guiding policy update to get more distinctive skills.

Use the skills learned by DIAYN as the low-level of HRL, and use a meta-controller to train the high-level policy, and achieve good performance in multiple tasks.

Forward form of the mutual information

A similar lower bound to that in Equation can be derived by expanding the forward form of the mutual information in Equation:

\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} I(S;Z)&=\mathbb{E}_{s,z\sim p(s,z)}[\log p(s\mid z)]-\mathbb{E}_{s\sim p(s)}[\log p(s)] \\ &\geq \mathbb{E}_{s,z\sim p(s,z)}[\log q_{\phi}(s\mid z)]-\mathbb{E}_{s\sim p(s)}[\log p(s)]\label{eq16} \end{aligned} \end{equation}

DADS is the method within this category, DADS follows a model-based setup where I(s′;z∣s)I(s';z\mid s)I(s′;z∣s) is maximized.

\begin{equation} \begin{aligned} I(s';z\mid s)&=H(s'\mid s)-H(s'\mid s,z) \\ &= \int p(z,s,s')\log \frac{p(s'\mid s,z)}{p(s'\mid s)}ds'dsdz \\ &= \mathbb{E}_{z,s,s'\sim p}[\log \frac{p(s'\mid s,z)}{p(s'\mid s)}] \\ &\geq \mathbb{E}_{z,s,s'\sim p}[\log \frac{q_{\phi}(s'\mid s,z)}{p(s'\mid s)}] \end{aligned} \end{equation}

After fitting qϕq_{\phi}qϕ​, we can optimize π\piπ to maximize the lower bound with a reward function log⁡qϕ(s′∣s,z)−log⁡p(s′∣s)\log q_{\phi}(s'\mid s,z)-\log p(s'\mid s)logqϕ​(s′∣s,z)−logp(s′∣s). However, log⁡p(s′∣s)\log p(s'\mid s)logp(s′∣s) is intractable to compute, so DADS approximate the reward function:

\begin{equation} ​ r_z(s,a,s')=\log\frac{q_{\phi}(s'\mid s,z)}{\sum^L_{i=1}q_{\phi}(s'\mid s,z_i)}+\log L, \quad z_i\sim p(z) \end{equation}

Where p(s′∣s)=∫p(s′∣s,z)p(z∣s)dz≈∫qϕ(s′∣s,z)p(z)dz≈1L∑i=1Lqϕ(s′∣s,zi)p(s'\mid s)=\int p(s'\mid s,z)p(z\mid s)dz\approx \int q_{\phi}(s'\mid s,z)p(z)dz\approx \frac{1}{L}\sum^L_{i=1}q_{\phi}(s'\mid s,z_i)p(s′∣s)=∫p(s′∣s,z)p(z∣s)dz≈∫qϕ​(s′∣s,z)p(z)dz≈L1​∑i=1L​qϕ​(s′∣s,zi​), LLL denotes the number of samples from the prior.

The overall structure and algorithm are shown in the Figure. In general, it consists of two parts (1) training skill dynamic (2) using skill dynamic to generate intrinsic reward to train policy. The agent π\piπ interacts with the environment to produce a transition s→s′s\to s's→s′. Intrinsic reward is computed by computing the transition probability under qqq for the current skill zzz, compared to random samples from the prior p(z)p(z)p(z).

In an unsupervised skill learning setup, it is important to optimize the primitives to be diverse. However, we argue that diversity is not sufficient for the learned primitives to be useful for downstream tasks. Primitives must exhibit low-variance behavior, which enables long-horizon composition of the learned skills in a hierarchical setup. We analyze the variance of the x-y trajectories in the environment, where we also benchmark the variance of the primitives learned by DIAYN.

Figure demonstrates that DADS, which optimizes the primitives for predictability and diversity, yields significantly lower-variance primitives when compared to DIAYN, which only optimizes for diversity. This is starkly demonstrated in the plots of X-Y traces of skills learned in different setups. Skills learned by DADS show significant control over the trajectories generated in the environment, while skills from DIAYN exhibit high variance in the environment, which limits their utility for hierarchical control.

From Figure, we find that the meta-controller is unable to compose the skills learned by DIAYN, while the same meta-controller can learn to compose skills by DADS to navigate the Ant to different goals. This result seems to confirm that the high variance of the DIAYN skills limits their temporal compositionality.

Challenge

Through the previous introduction, although HRL has many advantages, it is not difficult to see that there are still many problems. Several questions still remain to be answered:

Better management of the non-stationarity of the higher-level state transition functions

Although both HAC and HIRO use the re-label experience method to solve the non-stationary problem between different levels to a certain extent, the HIRO method is difficult to calculate and the convergence speed is full. HAC will cause the meta controller to tend to choose A more conservative goal. How to solve this changing MDP control problem and solve the non-stationary problem is still an urgent problem in HRL.

Learn hierarchies automatically}

It is not difficult to see from some previous work that HRL still needs to manually design a large number of hyper-parameters. At the same time, a large amount of HRL work is concentrated on a two-layer control structure, and only individual papers discuss the possibility of three or four levels. However, the number of structural levels or the number of steps still cannot achieve automatic learning.

More efficient exploration

One of the reasons why HRL can perform better than ordinary reinforcement learning is because the choice of goals can drive the agent to take more efficient and meaningful exploration.

Improve stability

HRL is more difficult to train than general reinforcement learning methods because of its structural complexity and the new instability introduced between levels.

At the same time, there are many parameters in HRL algorithm, which makes it more difficult to reproduce and apply. This is also a problem that needs to be considered.

Conclusion

This article mainly introduces the recent research on hierarchical deep reinforcement learning, and divides the current mainstream hierarchical frameworks into goal-based framework and temporal abstraction based framework. After analyzing the advantages and disadvantages of their respective mainstream methods, we found that HRL has superiority over ordinary reinforcement learning methods in solving complex problems and transferring skills, but because of its structural complexity and instability, more research and improvement are still needed.

entry.title: Learning to Predict Without Looking Ahead: World Models Without Forward Prediction
entry.content:
作者认为对未来环境变化的预估是提升人类生存可能性的重要能力，而这些能力不是通过传统的world model学习机制就能够学习出来的。一般的学习机制是让policy与环境进行交互，得到真实的环境数据，然后把真实的环境数据与world model预估出来的数据组合成监督数据进行学习。作者为了让提高policy的“生存能力“，在每一时间步给policy输入的环境状态并不一定是真实的，有可能是world model产生的（在预估的环境状态下做决策），作者称之为“observation dropout"。这是一个model-based RL的工作，和以往强调sample efficiency的工作不同，它的主要贡献是提出了新的应用world model到RL中的方式。

仅使用世界模型去训练的策略在直接运用在真实环境的时候也取得了不错的效果，不需要很准确的进行预测，模型虽然存在偏差，但是agent依然能够学到处理任务的能力，这种特征是隐式地，但很好理解。比如世界模型是有倾斜的，或者一些动力学并不准确，但agent依然能学到如何在这种情况下立杆的能力，这种能力本身可以在环境之中进行迁移，agent只需要在遇到真实环境的时候稍加修正即可。

entry.title: episodic RL
entry.content:
episodic RL主要针对的是强化学习中：SGD学习率小，更新缓慢；奖励sparse，和延时奖励，奖励信号传递缓慢这些原因，从而导致强化学习缓慢的问题。引入了非参数的储存memory，从而直接进行学习，加快速度和sample efficiency。

1. model-free episodic control

主要思想是记录state-action pair （s,a）出发的最高累计奖励。

不过这种方式其实存在问题，对于(s,a)的奖励 服从方差较大的分布的情况，储存最大值用于指导动作选择并非最优，但是文中也指出该算法是面向near-deterministic，希望环境的动态几乎确定，也就是(s,a)的奖励)几乎确定，这样的环境中exploitation比exploration更重要，这时该算法才比较有效。

针对内存有限和相似状态的泛化，采用淘汰访问频率最低的记忆，如果输入的状态对存在于memory中，就直接调用，不存在就取k近邻的均值。

同时这篇论文的实验也比较了不同的表示学习方法对结果影响也较大，这里不重点讨论，之后再研究。

2. neural episodic control

  • 提出一个可学习的端到端episodic control网络架构（Neural Episodic Control），使用了可差分的字典结构differentiable neural dictionary（DND）和可学习的卷积神经网络，储存使用的数据结构是td-tree；
  • 使用新状态的k近邻的值函数按照与新状态的距离成反比的权重求和估计新状态值函数。

这里的Q估计变为了N-step的形式，其余便是对于状态的表示行为进一步利用，针对h之间的距离，来表示不同状态之间的距离，从而对相似状态的估计进行权重均值计算。

3. episodic memory DQN

这里使用的random project 做的representation，这里利用情节记忆奖励对Q学习进行regularization：

  • 本算法为了快速获取key对应的值，没有采取k近邻取均值，而是直接取相等的k，这样的问题是一方面没有利用到状态之间的联系，另一方面对很多状态动作对直接使用历史数据的累积折扣奖励进行更新（无重要性采样），会由于策略之间的偏差造成最后结果有偏，不过使用一个较小的λ\lambdaλ能够缓解；
  • 本算法没有与neural episodic control等算法比较，实际上我认为只引入较小权重的H的表现应该不如neural episodic control；
  • 本文在两方面给了我们启发，一是non-parametric和parametric强化学习算法的结合，二是提供了一种新的使用与策略无关的累积折扣奖励（相当于MC的估计）结合TD的估计来提高off-policy的算法的样本利用效率的思路（因为off-policy中使用累积折扣奖励一般需要使用重要性采样）。

entry.title: 表示学习
entry.content:
为了提高机器学习系统的准确率，我们就需要将输入信息转换为有效的特征，或者更一般性称为表示（Representation）。如果有一种算法可以自动地学习出有效的特征，并提高最终机器学习模型的性能，那么这种学习就是可以叫做表示学习（Representation Learning）。

表示学习的关键是解决语义鸿沟（Semantic Gap）问题。语义鸿沟问题是指输入数据的底层特征和高层语义信息之间的不一致性和差异性。比如给定一些关于“车”的图片，由于图片中每辆车的颜色和形状等属性都不尽相同，不同图片在像素级别上的表示（即底层特征）差异性也会非常大。但是我们人理解这些图片是建立在比较抽象的高层语义概念上的。如果一个预测模型直接建立在底层特征之上，会导致对预测模型的能力要求过高。如果可以有一个好的表示在某种程度上可以反映出数据的高层语义特征，那么我们就可以相对容易地构建后续的机器学习模型。

在表示学习中，有两个核心问题：一是“什么是一个好的表示？”；二是“如何学习到好的表示？”

好的表示没有明确的标准，但通常具有以下几个优点：

  • 一个好的表示应该具有很强的表示能力，即同样大小的向量可以表示更多信息。
  • 一个好的表示应该使后续的学习任务变得简单，即需要包含更高层的语义信息。
  • 一个好的表示应该具有一般性，是任务或领域独立的。虽然目前的大部分表示学习方法还基于某个任务来学习，但我们期望学到的表示可以比较容易迁移到其它任务上。

在传统机器学习中，我们经常使用两种方式来表示特征：局部表示（Local Representation）和分布式表示（Distributed Representation）。

以颜色表示为例，一种表示颜色的方法是以不同名字来命名不同的颜色，这种表示方式叫做one-hot局部表示，也称为离散表示或符号表示。局部表示通常可以表示为向量的形式。

局部表示有两个不足之处：（1）one-hot 向量的维数很高，且不能扩展。如果有一种新的颜色，我们就需要增加一维来表示；（2）不同颜色之间的相似度都为0，即我们无法知道“红色”和“中国红”的相似度要比“红色”和“黑色”的相似度要高。

另一种表示颜色的方法是用RGB值来表示颜色，不同颜色对应R、G、B三维空间中一个点，这种表示方式叫做分布式表示。分布式表示通常可以表示为低维的稠密向量。

和局部表示相比，分布式表示的表示能力要比局部表示强很多，分布式表示的向量维度一般都比较低。我们只需要用一个三维的稠密向量就可以表示所有颜色。并且分布式表示也很容易表示新的颜色名。此外，不同颜色之间的相似度也很容易计算。

我们可以使用神经网络来将高维的局部表示空间映射到一个非常低维的分布式表示空间。在这个低维空间中，每个特征不再是坐标轴上的点，而是分散在整个低维空间中。

在机器学习中，这个过程也称为嵌入（Embedding）。嵌入通常指将一个度量空间中的一些对象映射到另一个低维的度量空间中，并尽可能保持不同对象之间的拓扑关系。比如自然语言中词的分布式表示，也经常叫做词嵌入。

要学习到一种好的高层语义表示（一般为分布式表示），通常需要从底层特征开始，经过多步非线性转换才能得到。一个深层结构的优点是可以增加特征的连续多次的线性转换等价于重用性，从而指数级地增加表示能力。因此，表示学习的关键是构建具有一定深度的多层次特征表示。

传统的特征学习一般是通过人为地设计一些准则，然后根据这些准则来选取有效的特征。特征的学习是和最终预测模型的学习分开进行的，因此学习到的特征不一定可以提升最终模型的性能。

entry.title: dynamic-aware unsupervised discovery of skills
entry.content:
学习一个整个状态空间中所包含的不同行为的全局动态模型是困难的，但是只学习一部分特定行为就变得简单了。如果我们可以学习行为和对应的动态信息，就能利用模型预测控制来在行为空间中进行plan，而不是在动作空间中进行planning。提出一种叫dynamic-aware discovery of skills（DADS），一种无监督RL框架，利用model-free的方法来学习低层次技能，旨在使model-based控制变得简单。

preliminaries

互信息可以当作鼓励探索的一个目标。I(X;Y)=H(X)−H(X∣Y)I(X;Y)=H(X)-H(X|Y)I(X;Y)=H(X)−H(X∣Y)，最大化互信息就是要最大化X的熵的同时最小化conditional entropy H(X∣Y)H(X|Y)H(X∣Y)。X一般是状态的函数，Y是动作的函数，最大化这个目标可以保证其探索性。

DADS

在（1）中的互信息可以显示在给定z和s的情况下，我们能知道多少关于s′s's′的信息，直观的，技能z可以视作是抽象的动作序列。我们想要优化我们基于技能的controller π(a∣s,z)\pi(a|s,z)π(a∣s,z)，可以重写公式2：

p(z,s,s′)=p(z)p(s∣z)p(s′∣s,z)p(z,s,s')=p(z)p(s|z)p(s'|s,z)p(z,s,s′)=p(z)p(s∣z)p(s′∣s,z),


这里使用了KL散度的非负性。技能动态q代表转换方程的变分近似。在每次迭代中：

最小化DKL(p(s′∣s,z)∣∣qϕ(s′∣s,z))D_{KL}(p(s'|s,z)||q_{\phi}(s'|s,z))DKL​(p(s′∣s,z)∣∣qϕ​(s′∣s,z))来收紧下限。对于神经网络近似，可以把梯度写为：

在fitting q之后，我们通过最大化Ez,s,s′[logqϕ(s′∣s,z)−logp(s′∣s)]E_{z,s,s'}[logq_{\phi}(s'|s,z)-logp(s'|s)]Ez,s,s′​[logqϕ​(s′∣s,z)−logp(s′∣s)]来优化π\piπ。这是一种RL优化器，因为logp(s′∣s)logp(s'|s)logp(s′∣s)不能直接计算，所以：

planning using skill dynamics

entry.title: accelerating online RL with offline datasets
entry.content:
对于数据驱动的强化学习，offline datasets 包括状态，动作和奖励的轨迹。这些数据可以是来自于所需任务的demonstrations，次级优化策略，相关任务的demonstrations，或者只是环境中的随机探索。取决于数据质量，关于动态模型的有用的先验知识可以从中提取出来。一种有效的数据驱动方式应该是可以利用这些数据去pre-train 离线，fine-tuning 在线。

这篇论文的方法是使用dynamic programming 训练一个critic，有监督的更新去训练一个actor。动态规划可以利用off-policy 数据和样本高效学习。监督actor更新隐式地向偏离分布的动作施加了限制。

preliminary

RL agent的目标就是最大化回报期望，可以利用策略梯度方法学习到最优策略，但是因为estimator 的高方差导致这种方法的低效。很多算法试图通过引入价值函数，Q函数，优势A函数来降低方差。

actor-critic算法通过两部分来最大化return：策略估计和策略提升。策略估计阶段，通过当前策略评估critic Q函数：

通过迭代Qk+1=BπQkQ^{k+1}=B^{\pi}Q^kQk+1=BπQk 使 QkQ^kQk 收敛至 QπQ^{\pi}Qπ。通过函数近似我们很难准确的估计bellman operator，所以来最小化bellman error：

在策略提升阶段，actor的策略基于当前Q值进行更新：

challenges in offline RL with Online Fine-tuning

problem definition

假设在训练之前提供一个关于转换信息的静态数据集，D={(s,a,s′,r)j}D=\{(s,a,s',r)_j\}D={(s,a,s′,r)j​}。数据集可以是一个随机策略的采样，也可以是一个专家行为。目标是利用D来进行pre-training，并且进行一些在线的交互来优化策略。

data efficiency

一种利用先验数据的方式是模仿学习pre-train一个policy然后对on-policy RL进行fine-tuning。但是这种方法有两个缺点：1. 先验数据可能不是最优 2. on-policy fine-tuning是低效的，其在RL阶段是不重用过往经验的。考虑到sample efficiency，使用off-policy的actor-critic算法。

bootstrap error in offline learning with actor-critic method

一个标准的off-policy actor-critic 算法应用在这个问题上时，表现并不理想，尽管有先验数据的存在，但算法并没有从离线的pre-train中得到明显的改善。这里尝试了SAC，可以看出前后并没有太大区别（figure 2）。论文提出这种问题可能是因为off-policy bootstrapping 的误差累积。一旦动作偏离分布，那么Q值也将不准确。为了解决这一问题，引入了限制：


这里的πθ\pi_{\theta}πθ​ 是需要更新的actor，πβ(a∣s)\pi_{\beta}(a|s)πβ​(a∣s)是过去所有收集数据所表示的分布。很多离线RL算法都是通过最大似然估计来fit 一个分布πβ\pi_{\beta}πβ​ 样本的参数模型：π^β=maxπ^βEs,a πβ[logπ^β(a∣s)]\hat{\pi}_{\beta}=max_{\hat{\pi}_{\beta}}E_{s,a~\pi_{\beta}}[log\hat{\pi}_{\beta}(a|s)]π^β​=maxπ^β​​Es,a πβ​​[logπ^β​(a∣s)]。估计完π^β\hat{\pi}_{\beta}π^β​，就能像公式5一样对其施加限制，方式很多，如在策略更新中加入惩罚项，或者一些动作采样的结构选择。但是这种准确估计使其很难和在线fine-tuning一起使用。

excessively conservative online learning

从图2我们不难看出，在在线阶段，性能很难得到明显的提升。主要的挑战是在线学习阶段收集的数据如何去fit 一个准确的行为模型，offline可以通过最大似然训练一个模型，但是在online阶段，行为模型必须实时跟进不断得到的数据。可见，这种限制优化过于保守，在新数据来临时并不能很好的fit，所以导致了这种limited improvement。所以这要求我们的算法既能限制策略来使其稳定避免累计误差，并且不能过于保守。

advantage weighted actor critic

提出了一种名叫advantage weighted actor critic （AWAC）算法，其中的actor引入了一种隐式地限制。AWAC学一个可以使在策略评估阶段学习到的critic价值最大化的策略，同时使策略分布与数据分布保持一致。在第k轮，优化策略使每个状态的Q值最大，并且限制数据分布与离线训练时相似。

之前已经提到了，用参数模型去限制这种明显的约束效果并不好，所以引入非参数的隐式地约束：

这个问题的近似形式解为：

我们需要将非参数解投影到策略空间中：

experiment

entry.title: Sample-Efficient Reinforcement Learning with Stochastic Ensemble Value Expansion
entry.content:
将model-free和model-based方法结合在一起是很有潜力的。model-based方法是建立一个环境的动态模型，利用动态模型训练一个策略on rollouts, 利用rollouts去提升TD learning，用从rollout中得到的信息作为策略的输入。但是这种方式在复杂的动态环境中很难学习到准确的模型，累计误差的产生会让策略错误。

这篇论文提出一种stochastic ensemble value expansion （STEVE）方法，对model-based value expansion（MVE）方法的变体。MVE推理未来的定长步数，但是这会积累模型误差。STEVE对不同长度进行插值，偏爱具有低不确定度的水平长度。为了计算插值目标，用ensemble替代模型和Q函数，计算采样的方差来表征ensemble的不确定度。

background

d(s′)d(s')d(s′) 如果 s′s's′ 是终态则为1，其余为0。 我们从replay buffer中均匀采样估计Q函数。

在计算TD target的时候使用的是旧的参数 θ−\theta^-θ− 。

因为我们计算的是连续动作空间，所以不能简单的计算 maxaQ^θπ(s,a)max_a\hat{Q}_{\theta}^{\pi}(s,a)maxa​Q^​θπ​(s,a)，利用一个神经网络去近似最大函数。

这篇论文将DDPG作为基础的学习算法。

MVE

动态模型有三个学习函数：转换函数T^\hat{T}T^，终止函数d^\hat{d}d^，返回是否是终态的可能性，和奖励函数。模型意图最小化：

若是离散环境中，第一项可以替换成cross entropy loss。

MVE发现从replay buffer中采样得到的Q函数误差比模型推理中得到的误差要低，他们将这个叫做分布偏差问题，并采用TD-k 作为解决方案。

Stochastic ensemble value expansion

H 的时间步长推理，我们可以计算出H+1个不同的目标，标准的TD learning是 T0MVE\Tau_0^{MVE}T0MVE​，MVE是 THMVE\Tau_H^{MVE}THMVE​。对所有的目标都进行插值，然后利用不同的权重来平衡误差。

algorithm

derivation

通过权重w来最小化mean-squared误差，可以通过ensemble的经验方差来估计方差项，但是对于偏差项并没有一个合适的estimator。所以这篇论文中，忽略了偏差，来最小化方差项：

experiments

entry.title: GhostNet: More Features from Cheap Operations
entry.content:
CNNs are very powerful, especially the emergence of resnet, which makes it possible for network performance to become better and better as the depth of the network becomes deeper, but this leads to a large and slow model. Recently, various researches have appeared in the design of lightweight networks, such as model pruning, quantification, knowledge distillation, or some simplified network structure design. Today I am going to introduce a paper on CVPR2020 from Huawei Noah Lab (I think this is designed for Huawei mobile phones). Ghostnet can achieve the same accuracy as SOTA algorithm with less computational complexity .

The paper provides a new Ghost module, which aims to generate more feature maps through cheap operations. Based on a set of original feature maps, the author applies a series of linear transformations to generate many "Ghost feature maps" that can extract the required information from the original features at a small cost. The Ghost module is plug-and-play, and Ghost bottleneck is obtained by stacking Ghost modules, and then a lightweight neural network-GhostNet is built.

A well-trained deep neural network usually contains rich or even redundant feature maps to ensure a comprehensive understanding of the input data. As shown in the figure above, in ResNet-50, the feature map after the first residual block processing is taken out, and the three similar feature maps are annotated with the same color box for the example. One feature map in the pair can be obtained by transforming the other feature map through a cheap operation (represented by a wrench), and one of the feature maps can be regarded as a "ghost" of the other. Because, this article proposes that not all feature maps need to be obtained by convolution operation, "ghost" feature maps can be generated by cheaper operations.

Ghost Module

Although recent work such as MobileNet and ShuffleNet introduced deep convolution or shuffling operations to use smaller convolution kernels (floating-number operations) to build effective CNNs, the remaining 1×11\times 11×1 convolutional layer will still occupy A lot of memory and FLOPs.

The author proposes to reduce the required resources, namely the convolution kernels used to generate them. In fact, given input data X∈Rc×h×wX\in R^{c\times h \times w}X∈Rc×h×w, where ccc is the number of input channels, hhh and www are heights and width of the input data, the operation of any convolutional layer to generate nnn feature maps can be expressed as

Y=X∗f+bY=X*f+b Y=X∗f+b

where ∗*∗ is the convolution operation, bbb is the bias term,Y∈Rh′×w′×nY∈R^{h′×w′×n}Y∈Rh′×w′×n is the output feature map with nnn channels, and f∈Rc×k×k×nf∈R^{c×k×k×n}f∈Rc×k×k×n is the convolution filters in this layer. In addition, h′h′h′ and w′w′w′ are the height and width of the output data, and k×kk×kk×k is the kernel size of convolution filters fff . During this convolution procedure, the required number of FLOPs can be calculated as n⋅h′⋅w′⋅c⋅k⋅kn·h′·w′·c·k·kn⋅h′⋅w′⋅c⋅k⋅k, which is often as large as hundreds of thousands since the number of filters nnn and the channel number care generally very large (e.g.e.g.e.g. 256 or 512).

According to the above formula, the number of parameters to be optimized (parameters in fff and bbb) is determined by the size of the input and output feature maps. As observed in Figure 1, the output feature map of the convolutional layer usually contains a lot of redundancy, and some of them may be similar to each other. The author pointed out that it is not necessary to use a large number of FLOPs and parameters to generate these redundant feature maps one by one. Instead, the output feature maps are "ghosts" of a few original feature maps through some cheap conversion. These original feature maps usually have a small size and are generated by ordinary convolution kernels. Specifically, mmm original feature maps Y′∈Rh′×w′×mY'\in R^{h'\times w' \times m}Y′∈Rh′×w′×m are generated using one convolution:

Y′=X∗f′Y'= X*f' Y′=X∗f′

where f′∈Rc×k×k×mf'\in R^{c\times k\times k\times m}f′∈Rc×k×k×m is the kernel of convolution, m<nm<nm<n, hyperparameters (such as convolution kernel size, stride, padding) are the same as those in ordinary convolution to keep the spatial size of the output feature map (i.e. hhh and w′w'w′ ) consistent. In order to further obtain the required nnn feature maps, the author proposes to apply a series of cheap linear operations to each original feature in Y′Y'Y′ to generate sss ghost feature maps:

yij=Φij(yi′),∀i=1,…,m,j=1,…,s,y_{ij}=\Phi_{ij}(y'_i), \forall i=1,\dots ,m, j=1,\dots,s, yij​=Φij​(yi′​),∀i=1,…,m,j=1,…,s,

where y′iy′_iy′i​ is the i−thi-thi−th intrinsic feature map in Y′Y′Y′, Φi,jΦ_{i,j}Φi,j​ in the above function is the j−thj-thj−th (except the last one) linear operation for generating the j−thj-thj−th ghost feature map yijy_{ij}yij​, that is to say, y′iy′_iy′i​ can have one or more ghost feature maps {yij}j=1s\{y_{ij}\}^s_{j=1}{yij​}j=1s​. The last Φi,sΦ_{i,s}Φi,s​ is the identity mapping for preserving the intrinsic feature maps as shown in Figure 2. We can obtain n=m⋅sn=m·sn=m⋅s feature maps Y=[y11,y12,⋅⋅⋅,yms]Y= [y_{11},y_{12},···,y_{ms}]Y=[y11​,y12​,⋅⋅⋅,yms​] as the output data of a Ghost module as shown in Figure 2. Note that the linear operations ΦΦΦ operate on each channel whose computational cost is much less than the ordinary convolution. In practice, there could be several different linear operations in a Ghost module, e.g.3×3e.g.3×3e.g.3×3 and 5×55×55×5 linear kernels, which will be analyzed in the experiment part.

Building Efficient CNNs

Experiments

I selected the cifar10 experiment in the original paper to reproduce it. According to the conclusion of the paper, select the kernel size of 3X3, the ratio s=2, and compare resnet56, vgg16, and with ghost module.

resnet56:

ghost_resnet56:

vgg16:

ghost_vgg16:

FLOPS:

)

flops without with ghost module
resnet56 126.55M 67.9M
vgg16 333.35M 178.55M

It is a pity that the accuracy in the paper is not reached, which may be the reason for some parameter selection.

You can check my code on https://github.com/Hotwaterman/CV_ghsotnet
